title: Clinicopathological characterization of non-guttata corneal endothelial dystrophy in Saudi patients with idiopathic endothelial failure.
abstract: Corneal endothelial cell dysfunction is a common cause of corneal decompensation in elderly. We describe a non-guttata corneal endothelial dystrophy with correlation of the clinical features to the histopathological and ultrastructural characteristics in a cohort of Saudi patients. A retrospective study of all consecutive cases of primary corneal decompensation in phakic eyes due to endothelial attenuation, in the absence of guttata is conducted. Patients were treated by either penetrating keratoplasty (PKP) or Descemet's Stripping Automated Endothelial Keratoplasty (DSAEK) as a primary procedure between 2002 and 2016. Clinical and demographic data were obtained through chart review and the histopathological data were collected by reviewing Descemet's membrane (DM) in the corneal tissue samples of the affected eyes. We included 17 eyes from 17 patients (10 females and 7 males), with a mean age of 67.17 ± 8.98 years. All patients were phakic, and decreased vision was the patients' main complaint at presentation. The pre-operative endothelial cell count was obtained in 3 eyes with a mean of 531.7 ± 309.5/mm. Histopathology of the 17 corneal specimens showed thick multi-laminated DM and attenuated endothelium. The success rate of the primary procedure was 70%. The cornea of the other eye remained clear in 12/17 patients. This is a type of corneal endothelial cell dysfunction with a late onset of presentation, spontaneous corneal decompensation in phakic eyes or a rapid onset of decompensation following uncomplicated surgery. It seems to be asymmetrically bilateral among our Saudi patients. Fellow eyes are at the same risk of decompensation. DM lacks the presence of guttata clinically and histopathologically.
full_text:
Clinicopathological characterization of non-guttata corneal endothelial dystrophy in Saudi patients with idiopathic endothelial failure

Corneal endothelial cell dysfunction is a common cause of corneal decompensation in elderly. We describe a non-guttata corneal endothelial dystrophy with correlation of the clinical features to the histopathological and ultrastructural characteristics in a cohort of Saudi patients. A retrospective study of all consecutive cases of primary corneal decompensation in phakic eyes due to endothelial attenuation, in the absence of guttata is conducted. Patients were treated by either penetrating keratoplasty (PKP) or Descemet’s Stripping Automated Endothelial Keratoplasty (DSAEK) as a primary procedure between 2002 and 2016. Clinical and demographic data were obtained through chart review and the histopathological data were collected by reviewing Descemet’s membrane (DM) in the corneal tissue samples of the affected eyes. We included 17 eyes from 17 patients (10 females and 7 males), with a mean age of 67.17 ± 8.98 years. All patients were phakic, and decreased vision was the patients’ main complaint at presentation. The pre-operative endothelial cell count was obtained in 3 eyes with a mean of 531.7 ± 309.5/mm2. Histopathology of the 17 corneal specimens showed thick multi-laminated DM and attenuated endothelium. The success rate of the primary procedure was 70%. The cornea of the other eye remained clear in 12/17 patients. This is a type of corneal endothelial cell dysfunction with a late onset of presentation, spontaneous corneal decompensation in phakic eyes or a rapid onset of decompensation following uncomplicated surgery. It seems to be asymmetrically bilateral among our Saudi patients. Fellow eyes are at the same risk of decompensation. DM lacks the presence of guttata clinically and histopathologically.

Introduction

The cornea is one of the major refractive surfaces of the eye and its clarity is a pre- requisite for the optimal vision and function. Endothelial cells of the cornea play the major role in maintaining the corneal clarity and edema-free status through their pump system. Variable diseases can affect those cells, like surgeries, degenerations, dystrophies, trauma and viruses, leading to corneal decompensation and bullous keratopathy. The endothelium is a very thin layer, composed of monolayer of cuboidal cells attached to Descemet’s membrane (DM), and it does not have the privilege of cells proliferation, like the epithelium. The endothelium plays a major role in the corneal transparency and optimal function through the pump performance that regulates fluid access through the cornea and acts as barriers. In the conditions that may cause endothelial cells damage such as dystrophies, secondary to aging, or following surgical interventions, the remaining healthy cells migrate to cover the area of lost or damaged endothelium. Depending on the severity of the condition and the amount of involvement/loss of the cells, the endothelium may eventually fail to compensate for the damaged cells, and decompensation of the cornea becomes evident.

Corneal dystrophies constitute a progressive, genetically predisposed and bilateral heterogenous group. They are well described causes for vision loss, and they vary in their location, histopathology, clinical manifestations, and impact on vision. Endothelial dystrophies mainly involve DM and the endothelium. They usually impact vision later in life, initially necessitating medical treatment, but eventually requiring surgical intervention. The International Classification of Diseases (ICD) has labelled and recognized different endothelial dystrophies, like corneal Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy (FED), posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy and congenital hereditary endothelial dystrophy. FED has a complex patho-etiology with combined genetic and non-heritable risk factors. It is characterized by abnormal hexagonal endothelial cells morphology and eventual cell death associated with emerging excrescences or guttata that originate first centrally then progress depending on the stage of the dystrophy with eventual corneal decompensation. However, Brooks and his coauthors in 1990 nicely demonstrated and analyzed 17 cases of bullous keratopathy developing either spontaneously or soon after intraocular surgery due to what they have described as “non-guttata endothelial dystrophy”. They demonstrated two different groups of patients: one with abnormal morphology of the endothelium and considerably reduced endothelial cell count and another with normal endothelial cell count but significant pleomorphism and polymegathism representing late and early stages of this proposed dystrophy. Lee and his co-authors more than 25 years later described an idiopathic corneal decompensation observed as a unilateral condition, thus described as a degeneration.

In the present study, we describe similar condition where the endothelium is attenuated causing a primary corneal decompensation in a cohort of Saudi phakic patients who are not suspected to have ocular pathology or previous surgeries to explain the development of corneal edema. The complete absence of guttata defies the diagnosis of FED according to the ICD classification. The appearance of DM and the endothelium by histopathological examination and ultrastructural studies in these cases is characteristic and is almost identical to the idiopathic degenerative corneal decompensation reported by Lee and his co-authors in 2018; however, our findings support defining this condition as a true dystrophy.

Materials and methods

We have screened all corneal specimens obtained from Saudi patients who were operated either by penetrating keratoplasty (PKP) or Descemet’s Stripping Automated Endothelial Keratoplasty (DSAEK) to treat bullous keratopathy from 2002 to 2016. Patients who underwent a prior surgical intervention, or had a relevant ocular history that might attribute to the corneal decompensation (trauma, long term contact lens wear, uveitis, previous history of acute glaucoma, exfoliation/pseudo-exfoliation (PEX) syndrome, and confirmed herpetic eye disease) were excluded. All patients who had confirmed or suspected FED clinically and/or histopathologically were also excluded. Twenty corneal and DM specimens from 17 patients met the inclusion criteria in the mentioned period. Those 17 phakic patients who developed spontaneous, unexplained corneal decompensation and bullous keratopathy without previous surgical intervention were included and further studied. A follow- up period under the anterior segment and cornea service provided by different consultants of a minimum one year was also set in our inclusion criteria. Charts were reviewed for patients’ demographics, medical and surgical history, clinical presentation, ocular examination (visual acuity, intra-ocular pressure (IOP), and slit lamp examination), investigations, surgical interventions, outcome, and follow up. The histopathological slides were reviewed by an ocular pathologist to aid in the exclusion of cases with suspected early FED and in the description of DM in addition to the endothelial cells assessment. Outcomes were tracked for a follow up period that ranged from one year and up to 15 years with a mean of 6.12 years and a median of 4 years.

The study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki as amended in 2013. The research project was granted an expedited approval as a retrospective type of study by the Human Ethics Committee/Institutional Review Board (HEC/IRB) at King Khaled Eye Specialist Hospital with an approved collaborative agreement with King Abdulaziz University Hospital (RP 2064-R). A written general informed consent was obtained from all participants for the anonymous use and publication of their data and images. Neither drug trials nor human transplantation were involved in our study.

Data management and analysis plan

The 17 patients’ data were retrospectively collected from their medical records. All data were gathered using a data collection sheet that was designed by the researchers specifically for the purpose of this study. Data were then cleaned, managed, and coded using Microsoft Excel 2019® (Microsoft Corporation, Redmond, Washington, USA). Categorical data were presented as frequency and percentage, while continuous data were presented as mean ± SD. Paired t-test was used to assess the change in visual acuity and refraction. The Statistical Package for Social Sciences (IBM SPSS Statistics for Windows, Version 28.0. Armonk, NY: IBM Corp.) was used for the statistical analysis of patients’ retrieved data.

Results

Demographic and clinical aspects

The current study included 17 patients (10 females and 7 males), with a mean age of 67.17 ± 8.98 years. The right eye was affected in 10 cases, while the left was affected in seven cases. The main complaint of all patients at presentation was decreased vision, with no specific significant past surgical, traumatic or ocular medical history. All presenting eyes in the 17 patients were phakic: 16/17 presented with cataract, and one with a clear lens. Table 1 summarizes the demographics and clinical history in these 17 patients. PEX was absent in all patients. Corneal sensation was normal in 16 patients, and slightly decreased in two, nevertheless, were not confirmed to be herpetic based on a negative Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) testing and had not clinically showed any suggestive signs of viral disease. The clinical appearance of both eyes in two different patients is shown in (Fig. 1).

F: The clear cornea, dilated pupil, and immature cataract of the fellow eye in the same patient.

The visual acuity at presentation in the affected eye varied from 20/70 to hand movement. The patients’ IOP ranged from 10 to 23 mmHg (Mean ± SD: 17 ± 3.3 mmHg). The preoperative endothelial cell count was available and recorded in three patients revealing marked reduction in the endothelial cell count with a mean ± SD of 531.7 ± 309.5/mm2.

Management and outcome

Initially, the 17 patients who met our inclusion criteria were managed medically, but eventually all patients required corneal transplant by the primary treating anterior segment/cornea consultants following the standard techniques, that included full thickness PKP in 9 patients and partial thickness DSAEK in eight patients. Sixteen patients had combined cataract extraction along with keratoplasty in the form of extra- capsular cataract extraction (ECCE), or phacoemulsification with posterior chamber intra-ocular lens (PC IOL) implantation, while one patient had PKP only.

The patients were followed up post operatively for up to 15 years. The initial graft remained clear in 71%, while 29% had failed grafts with a variable onset. Repeated keratoplasty was conducted for three eyes (18%), one eye underwent DSAEK, while two eyes underwent PKP. After repeated keratoplasty, one eye was clear, while failure occurred in two eyes. Post-operatively, one eye developed progressive glaucoma. No further surgeries for visual rehabilitation were done as summarized in Table 2. The patients’ final post-operative visual acuity during the follow-up is shown in Graph 1.

Post-operative clinical course and outcome following first and repeated surgery.

Histopathology and ultrastructural findings

All corneal specimens underwent histopathology examination. The specimens were grossly obtained either from PKP procedures as full thickness corneal buttons with an average diameter of 7.5 mm or during DSAEK procedure, and they consist of DM. The full thickness buttons showed typical bullous keratopathy changes with epithelial intracellular edema, numerous intraepithelial bullae, sub-epithelial bullae and/or areas of regenerating epithelium (Fig. 2A). The basement membrane of the epithelium was irregular with areas of excess basement membrane production and formation of subepithelial fibrosis in some cases. Bowman’s layer was mostly intact, and it showed focal interruptions in few cases. The corneal stroma was thickened with generalized edema and variable alteration of the normal stromal lamellar architecture. DM in these corneas as well as in DSAEK specimens was thickened and multi-laminated with or without the presence of a posterior fibrillar collagenous layer, and it showed markedly attenuated endothelium (Figs. 2B & C). Ultrastructural studies showed thick multilayered DMs with the collagenous layer posterior to the non-banded DM zone posteriorly. This is demonstrated in (Fig. 3).

The measured visual acuity after surgery in our 17 patients (VA: visual acuity; NA: not available).

B: Higher power appearance of the posterior part of the same cornea showing thick multi-laminated DM and absent endothelium (Original magnification x400 Periodic acid Schiff). C: DM obtained during stripping procedure, which is similarly thick, and multi-laminated with fibrillar layer (black arrows) and absent endothelium (Original magnification x400 Periodic acid Schiff).

A: Transmission electron microscopy scan of one of the corneas posteriorly demonstrating thick multilayered Descemet’s membrane (DM) with collagenous material behind the non-banded zone of DM (White arrow) and abnormal endothelium (black arrowhead). B: Another scan showing similar stromal keratocyte (White arrowhead) and similar collagen posteriorly, posterior to the non-banded zone of DM (White arrow).

Condition of fellow eyes

Upon the follow- up of our 17 patients during the period of the current study and after the end of data collection until the submission of this study, we checked the status of the second eye in the same patients. Twelve patients (71%) had clear corneas in the fellow eyes, thus no further intervention was required. The status of the cornea of the fellow eyes is outlined in Table 3. The cornea in three patients decompensated soon after simple uncomplicated phacoemulsification and remained decompensated requiring PKP or DSAEK. These eyes were managed by PKP in two, and one DSAEK. One patient had history of primary corneal edema along with cataract at initial presentation elsewhere 6 years before the presentation to us, for which combined DSAEK, phacoemulsification, and PC IOL was performed. Interestingly, one fellow eye spontaneously decompensated three years after the initial presentation of the first eye.

Discussion

Endothelial dysfunction or damage is a common cause of corneal decompensation and visual impairment in elderly patients. The patho-etiology for the development of corneal edema is since corneal endothelial cells have limited regenerative capacity, thus they are compensated by the spreading of the existing endothelial cells to cover the posterior aspect of the cornea. This affects the pump function, leading to edema which can alter the spacing of the stromal collagen fibers and reduce the overall corneal transparency. Several studies reported that people older than 40 years old and women have a higher risk of developing corneal endothelial dystrophy. Islam et al. added that the normal range of corneal endothelial cell density at birth is 4000–5000 cells/mm2, but with aging, it declines with the rate of 0.3–0.6%/year to an average of 2000–3000 cells/mm2 in normal adult eyes. Other causes for endothelial cell dysfunction include viral infections such as mumps and cytomegalovirus endothelialitis, in addition to herpetic kerato-uveitis. In our study group, even though no aqueous humor samples were taken to be tested for possible viral etiology, the possibility of herpetic viral infection has been ruled out based on ocular history, clinical examination (with intact corneal sensation), and negative PCR. Interestingly, Ahuja et al. noted that in Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy (FED), corneal sensitivity usually decreases and remains subnormal even years after endothelial keratoplasty. They attributed the decreased sensitivity, as being secondary, to the loss of sub-basal nerves and nerve abnormal morphology, which may persist after endothelial keratoplasty. At presentation, our patients’ main complaint was decreased vision, which is the hallmark of FED presentation as well. Hatou et al.explained that, as endothelial dystrophy progresses in FED, the endothelial function deteriorates, and corneal edema intensifies leading to a significant decline in visual acuity. The demographics in our cases showed female preponderance with a female to male ratio of 3:2. Similarly, Sarnicola et al. reported that FED has a female to male ratio of 2.5:1, and no specific reasons for this female predilection could be highlighted.

It seems that the onset of the disease in our cases when compared to FED patients, is relatively delayed with a mean age of 67.2 ± 8.7 years while FED might appear earlier; however, in some reports, most of FED patients do not require surgical intervention until their 6th or even 7th decades of life. However, the clinical behavior of the endothelial dysfunction in our cohort of patients seems to be different, and the histopathological and ultrastructural findings have excluded the diagnosis of FED.

The family history of our patients was negative for any similar condition in any of the relatives, thus a peculiar mode of inheritance in relation to this type of endothelial dysfunction was not suspected.

Regarding the clinical associations, none of our patients was wearing a contact lens, and patients with confirmed PEX were excluded. Fontana et al. stated that pathogenesis of PEX is multifactorial. It has been suggested that PEX is a form of elastosis resulting from the increased production of some elastic micro-fibrillar components like fibrillin-1. The systemic origin of the disorder indicates why patients with unilateral involvement express signs of PEX in the unaffected fellow eye later, suggesting that PEX is a generalized disorder with an asymmetrical clinical presentation. Nevertheless, endothelial compromise related to PEX is well documented, but it doesn’t typically present with corneal edema and secondary decompensation without any intervention in phakic eyes, as their alteration and effect on endothelium are not severe unlike endothelial dystrophies where damage is significant. Aoki T et al. in 2020 demonstrated a significant association between the severity of PEX and the low endothelial cell density. They also suggested that the accumulation of PEX material leads to an early endothelial cell dysfunction.

The earliest description of the proposed condition in our study was more than forty years ago. Abbott et al. in 1981 described a “non-guttata corneal endothelial degeneration” along with changes in the basement membrane (representing DM). His patients showed unilateral spontaneous corneal edema associated with severe attenuation of the endothelium, areas showing total endothelial cell loss and absent guttata. Brooks and his group in 1990 described a non-guttata type of bullous keratopathy in the absence of a relevant history of ocular disease. They described 17 patients with an evidence of bilateral endothelial dysfunction and morphology changes that resulted in corneal decompensation spontaneously either in association with an actual loss of the endothelial cells (in two patients) or without a reduced number of endothelial cells (in four patients). Eleven of those patients showed significant to mild abnormal morphology, represented by pleomorphism and polymegathism. The abnormal morphology was thought to result in increasing the susceptibility of these corneas to surgical stress leading to decompensation following surgery. They proposed this entity as a “non-guttata endothelial dystrophy” based on the bilateral findings in their patients. They also suggested that the significant loss of endothelial cells in some of their patients represented a late advanced stage of the disease. The most recent report found to be informative and interesting is the one presented by Lee and his group 28 years later. They described a similar group of patients with progressive idiopathic unilateral corneal endothelial failure of unknown etiology in phakic eyes. They had comprehensive demonstration of their cases and ruled out other possible causes for unilateral endothelial cell disease and/or loss (congenital hereditary endothelial dystrophy, iridocorneal endothelial syndrome, viral etiology or PEX material). In their histopathological description of eight corneal specimens, they similarly described the intracytoplasmic corneal epithelial swelling, irregularity, and bullous changes, associated with sub-epithelial excess basement membrane in all patients, along with fibrosis in some cases which has been also found in our full thickness PKP corneal specimens. DM of the corneal specimens in their study was thickened and multilayered histopathologically with an endothelial cell loss and absence of guttata. The analysis of transmission electron microscopy showed thick DM and variable deposition of thick collagen layer behind the posterior non-banded zone. Additional features in some patients were also noted which showed re-duplication of banded and non-banded zones of DM. The histopathological and ultrastructural findings in our cases are identical to those described and demonstrated by Lee and his group. The authors considered the pathological condition in their cases as an idiopathic unilateral disease because none of their patients developed similar corneal decompensation in the fellow eye within a follow-up period reaching up to 13 years. It is possible that none of their patients underwent cataract surgery in the fellow eye during that period to demonstrate the early post-operative decompensation observed in our cohort in three patients. This observation and the theory of susceptibility of the abnormal endothelial cells to “surgical stress” that was raised by Brooks in addition to the asymmetrical presentation between the two eyes noted in our cases all support the diagnosis of “non-guttata endothelial dystrophy” in our cohort of patients, that is yet to be further studied genetically. This is also further supported by the status in two of our patients, one who had history of spontaneous corneal decompensation with cataract 6-years earlier in one eye, which was not included in our study (necessitating DSAEK along with the cataract extraction) and another patient who had spontaneous corneal decompensation of the fellow eye 3 years after the first included study eye.

Management of our patients was mainly done by PKP, extracapsular cataract extraction (ECCE) and PC IOL implantation, while other more recent cases underwent DSAEK, phacoemulsification and PC IOL implantation. The success rate of the primary keratoplasty and the repeated grafts (in three patients only) was the same (70%). Price et al. has mentioned that the definitive treatment for corneal endothelial dysfunction is corneal transplant. Quek et al. added that symptomatic corneal edema has been treated successfully with endothelial or PKP. However the most significant risk factor for failure of keratoplasty is prior glaucoma filtration surgery, which accelerates the endothelial cell loss. Similar surgical procedures have been also used for the management of FED cases. PKP and DSAEK are the definitive treatments to restore vision. However, PKP has several potential complications, e.g., significant postoperative astigmatism, prolonged visual recovery, suture trauma, persistent epithelial defects, and recurrent refractive surgery. Therefore, other surgical techniques, such as DSAEK, Descemet’s membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK), posterior lamellar keratoplasty and deep lamellar endothelial keratoplasty are being conducted.

Price et al. reported that 10-year PKP survival rates reach 67–90% in FED patients; however, they get lower in pseudophakic bullous keratopathy (PBK). In one study enrolling > 1000 PKP recipients for endothelial dysfunction, survival rates at ten years were 80% in Fuch’s dystrophy while they reached 63% in patients with PBK and aphakia. Annette J et al.. were able to achieve the best corrected acuity of 20/40 in 37.5% after observing around 100 patients post triple procedures of PKP, ECCE and IOL implantation: 44 were FED patients and the others involved different pathologies like trauma, herpes and other endothelial dystrophy. Interestingly six patients were labelled as “non-guttata corneal endothelial dystrophy”. DMEK and DSAEK alone or with phacoemulsification have been adapted recently to treat such cases due to the documented advantages and safety profile. Success rate in one study of > 2000 patients of FED who underwent DMEK and DSAEK procedures reached more than 90% at 5 years in both groups. Interestingly, it has been documented in some studies that the rate of endothelial cell loss between PKP and DSEAK is similar at 10 years with 78% and 71%, respectively. Although the success rates have varied among different interventions and studies yet, outcomes are generally good. The most common causes for PKP failure are endothelial decompensation following an immunologic rejection episode and late endothelial decompensation.

The limitations of our study include the retrospective nature and the inability to perform specular microscopy testing for all patients because of the poor view. However, based on the previous studies that are suggestive of a non-guttata endothelial dystrophy (or degeneration) and the similar identical findings in our cases, we strongly propose further efforts in studying this entity genetically in our Saudi population.

Conclusions

In conclusion, we provide valuable observations of a “non-guttata endothelial dystrophy” among Saudis that seems to manifest asymmetrically in both eyes with relatively late onset of presentation in the late sixties or older. The endothelial cell count in these cases might or might not be low at presentation in both eyes as previously reported, but it seems to be vulnerable to even uncomplicated surgical stress, thus presenting with decompensation soon after surgery. DM in these cases has a peculiar thickened multi-laminated or multilayered appearance with no evidence of guttata. The need to recognize it in the first affected eye at initial presentation is significant to have a better management plan for the fellow eye when a patient develops cataract, keeping in mind that post-operative corneal decompensation is expected and inevitable soon after surgery. Such patients might be better treated by primary combined procedure since the definitive treatment for the corneal endothelial dysfunction in general is keratoplasty. This management plan may save patients from the morbidity of undergoing two separate surgical interventions. This condition is not well-established and is worth further investigations. There is a remote possibility of this entity being a non-guttata FED variant, thus a careful selection of patients for further genetic testing screening for FED might be useful.
